reasoning:
The question asks if there will be more protests in Kiribati in the 30 days before each of four resolution dates compared to the 30-day average over the 360 days preceding the forecast date (June 22, 2025). As of June 12, 2025, the 30-day average was exactly 0.000, indicating there had been zero ACLED-recorded protests in Kiribati over the preceding year.

A comprehensive search of news and ACLED reports from 2024 and 2025 revealed no evidence of protests in Kiribati since the June 12 data point (nor before). In a country with a very small population (~130,000) and a culture where public demonstrations are exceedingly rare, the baseline rate of protests is extremely low. Even an optimistic estimate of 1 protest every 5 years would mean a roughly 1-2% baseline probability for any 30-day window.

Given the absence of any recent triggering events (the last major political event was the August 2024 election, and no protests were recorded) and no news of unrest leading up to late June 2025, the probability of one or more protests occurring in the immediate 30-day window is very low. In adherence to the minimum probability bound of 5% and the principle that uncertainty should slightly increase for the more distant forecast horizons, the probabilities are set at the lower bound, marginally drifting upward over time.
